speaker: interviewee
text: The clock-- he just stops it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something. Going back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Boy, this is really something. You're incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm incredible?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Mrs. Breder, you showed us your number. And if you want to keep describing the significance of those numbers for us and where those numbers were-- how they became those numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I mentioned before, after, you know, delousing and cleansing us of-- what they said we are-- dirty Jews. So from this time on, our life in the camp started. We didn't have any idea of what's awaiting for us. And soon after, we were cleansed. Two days later, another transport came from Slovakia. And they kept coming, and coming, and coming. The 10 barracks were full. And then in 1942, also started coming transports from Poland, from-- I think so-- after the Slovakian transport came, from all over Europe-- I mean, Poland, Romania, Yugoslavia-- from Litva, from Latvia, you-- you name it. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the barrack like physically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically, the barrack was a two-story building, brick building. Like I said, they were built before the war-- World War II-- 1917, or something like that. And they were built for armies, like army barracks. They were-- they were converted as prison camps, I think so-- 1939 when Hitler came-- occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And first in those barracks were Polish prisoner-- I mean, political prisoners, not soldiers. Then when they captured the Russians, you know, when war started, 1939 in Poland, and then with Russia, 1941, yes? Then, the Russian-- because right from the start, you know, the-- the Russians were not prepared. So, you know, masses were captured of Russian POWs. And they were placed there in Birkenau, which I will come later to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were killed plainly. They starved them to death. And they were in condition indescribable, which we experienced later, in 1942. And the transport, like I say, kept-- kept coming, and coming, and coming-- unbelievable. But we didn't work yet. Two or three weeks later, they started to form-- it was already-- I think so-- April. I don't know-- center-- maybe around middle of April, for working units. So after the roll call, they-- they chopped up let's say, 200, 300, and to different places. We had to march out from Auschwitz, and march many kilometers to a working place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was assigned to a working place where we had to spread manure on the fields. Like I told you, we didn't have shoes, only wooden clogs, and nothing to cover our hands, and no tools, no shovels, nothing. So from the pile of-- of manure, we had to pick it up by hand and spread it on the frozen field. The field was still frozen, And it was snowing! But we had to go in-- in schnell. The SS guards with dogs were after us-- and schnell, schnell.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when the sun came out, the soil started melting. And the wooden clogs were stuck in it. So we had to go barefoot and rush-- and from the pile, spreading, and back, and back, and there. It was murder! At noontime, they brought a kettle of soup to the working place. So you line up. And the kapo, the German kapo, with the big ladle, you know, and we had the red bowl with-- with-- how you say-- [NON-ENGLISH]. With a--

speaker: interviewer
text: String.

speaker: interviewee
text: --string fastened to our, you know, waist, and the spoon hidden here somewhere. Because when you lose it, you never get it. If you lost your bowl, you didn't eat. So to stay in line for a half an hour-- we had a half an hour pause-- and get a little [LAUGHS] soup. It was garbage, not soup. The soup consisted of-- of potato peels-- it was a dark soup-- and a little vegetable, the white vegetable. I don't know how you call it. We called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was lukewarm. And everybody wanted to get. We were thirsty and hungry. So the last in the row hardly get it. But I tell you. I told you already, I have two elbows, and I'm a fighter. I always managed to get the soup. But I lost one of my clogs, so I had one and the other barefoot. But we huddled together, the half an hour. You know, the little soup, it was-- shk-- and it was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened? Who couldn't run too fast and couldn't make it, he was shot on the place. We had to pull the corpse aside until we go back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we lined up-- before dawn, I would said-- to go back to the camp. They counted us-- the SS, you know, on both sides with-- with the-- their dogs. So the last row had to pull the corpse-- grab by the hand, you know, and-- two-- one at one hand-- and pulled the corpse back to the camp, because they had to be counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you imagine? And it was a dirt road, not paved roads at all. Until we came back, the roads were-- sparkle? No sparkle-- I don't know the expression-- the term full of blood. And the corpse didn't have clothing, neither did they have skin anymore on their backs. And this was done all the time, all the time was- when I was working there-- about two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, you know, when they started forming commandos, I tried to hide between the new arrivals, which still didn't go to work. So I wouldn't have to go. But later on-- I worked there about two weeks. It was horrible, I tell you. I got-- I was lucky. I was assigned to a special unit, about 300 girls of us. And this unit was called Canada. We were assigned to do-- to sort the clothing after people-- you know-- people still didn't go to the gas chambers in 1942, because they were not built. In 1942, you know, it was already in one barrack the piles of-- of their luggage and the clothing, which we undressed. And we-- our job was to sort the clothing, and you know, separate the underwear, panties, and so on, and bundle them neatly in 10 in a package. And then, you know, the other barrack was where we loaded those packages. When the transport came, this time to Auschwitz, not to Birkenau-- we were still in Auschwitz-- those packages, we had to [NON-ENGLISH]-- take to those empty cattle cars where they brought the new arrivals. And on the-- I mean, outside, on those cars, were letters, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter help for the families who have their son on the front. So this Jewish clothing for-- from us, which they brought, you know, in quotation 'to work,' went back to Germany, warmed the families, which actually sent us to our deaths.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irony was-- irony. But you know, some of the-- us, right in the beginning-- very brave girls. I didn't do it right in the beginning-- wrote a little, because we had everything-- pencils. There were paper, writing paper-- wrote down when the SS didn't look, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. How did they write that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish clothing from concentration camp-- stacked in in between, because they didn't open-- they didn't even think of it at this time, writing this. So we hoped somebody got it in the pocket, in these, when they got the nice clothing. They didn't tell them-- the Germans-- where the clothing come from. And you have no idea what people brought along-- brought jewelry, furs, the best from the best. But because when you go somewhere, sure, you take the best, either of selling or to trading for food. You know how it went. But then end of August, Auschwitz women's camp, with the 10 barracks-- and people were living outside, too. It was so crowded you had to step over people who were sitting outside. Relocated us-- we marched from Auschwitz to a new camp, to Birkenau. Birkenau was five kilometers from Auschwitz. We thought that Auschwitz was hell. We didn't know what Birkenau is waiting for us. We came in to Birkenau in the summer. Winter was bad. Summer was worse. It was so hot in summer. Our heads, shaved heads-- blisters everywhere. The feet swollen-- full of-- of blisters on the feet, and-- and you know, cuts. Because you know, the shoes we didn't have. The hunger, Muselmann there, you know, just dragging our bodies-- not me, because I was lucky. I could steal food from the luggage, from the-- from the packages, what they brung along-- and brought-- under the roof. I had water there. I am talking generally. It's not mine what went through, but we lived in the same place with the rest of the population, the same barrack. So naturally, we sort everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they called our commander the elite commander, because we looked fat and pretty clean, yes? We could steal everything. But we stole in the morning when we went to the barrack. We put on five underwears. We came to-- to-- the camp, and we gave it to another girl, or whoever came. So we exchanged, you know. So they have something, the lucky ones. You know how many things can you bring? 300 when you have thousands and thousands. But I mean, then in Birkenau, when we came, we marched in. It was, you know, no road-- no-- lots of dust. You didn't see a green leaf-- nothing. Like I said, the Russian POWs who were there-- so they were tortured. So what? They ate in the grass-- so did the inmates, you know, when we came. So it was bare.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in Auschwitz-- it's in Birkenau, the soil is clay. In the summer, it hardens like cement. In-- later on when the-- when the rain started, when you walked in it, the clay sucked your foot in. And you had a hard time to pull out your foot. It was no road, no nothing. In the middle was a huge-- like a dirt road. On one side-- on the left side were brick barracks, on the right side, wooden barracks. But in 19-- end of 1942, were already non-Jewish inmates there, because Polish from Montelupich, from the prison in-- you know, Polish students-- and from other countries-- communists. So they were also. So the Jews were-- were housed in the brick barracks and the non-Jews in the wooden barracks. The brick barrack, when we came in, it was, you know, they called it [NON-ENGLISH]-- around the wall. And next to the wall make like bunks-- but one-- you know, three bunks. It was no ceiling, just open, everything. Clay-- the soil-- I mean inside was nothing, just clay. And the lower [NON-ENGLISH] was completely dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say there was no roof?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was roof, but it was not ceiling. We didn't have a ceiling. Just was open-- the wood, you can see the wood I mean. And the second was dark. Only the top was light where you could stretch out. But in the two down, you had to lie down. I was lucky. Not I-- I jumped to the third floor-- and with whom we were good friends-- about nine of us-- because to one [NON-ENGLISH], where only three or four people could sleep, we were assigned 10. Because in one barrack were 1,000 inmates. So the upper [NON-ENGLISH] was lucky-- but no mattress, no straw, and no blanket-- only the worn-out Russian uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We, you know, when we came to-- to the camp, had to live in-- in our working place. We could change to [NON-ENGLISH] clothing. But we had to leave them there and then put up our uniforms, the Russian-- when we went back to camp. So you know, there we changed. And they enclosed our uniform at the working place. And they enclosed the-- the Russian uniforms. And when we went back to the camp, we had to change. So we came to Birkenau still in our Russian uniforms. And we didn't go to work, only then after we moved there, after maybe two, three days. And this time, we were already close to 50,000, or maybe more, women. But people, what they did-- many people in Auschwitz, on the second floor, jumped to their death. Many committed suicide going to the high voltage. In the morning, you see like a Christmas tree. People you know, charred, dark, hanging on to-- to-- to the, you know, high voltage. And when we came to Birkenau, it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: No-- in Auschwitz, down in the basement, I said it was dripping, the water. There was no water. No toilets. Latrine was 200 feet behind the barrack. Can you imagine thousands of girls going to the latrine? The latrine was a open hole-- nothing-- only boards around them-- and the kapo standing in the entrance, because it was in also a fenced-up thing. And you have time-- only five minutes, not even. [NON-ENGLISH]. Everybody wanted to go. When they caught you on the way, they killed you. What do you think? You know how many people she drowned in the waste? And we had to pull them out for the say appell. You know, just describing that, a normal person-- you can't comprehend that. How? Only we who were there. Luckily, some of us still remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Getting out-- you know, I feel like I would be right away there. I can see the barrack. I can see the latrine. And I feel already the smell. But I wanted so badly to live. Many gave up-- many, many. And mainly, I tell you which-- from which part of Europe. When the people, the transport from Holland came-- the transport-- those girls were not used to harsh life. Like we were already four years, since 1938. I mean, not in condition like that-- well, but we experienced hardship-- but not in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were dying like flies. They couldn't take it. They mostly committed suicide. And they didn't want to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Greek girls also-- and very, you know, durable, where, you know, they said that we are like Kazakhs. We didn't-- we wanted to survive. We were strong in will. And very good the Polish girls, even better, because they experience hardship before. And you know, from Yugoslavia-- very, very strong people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were fighting for our lives every minute, every minute, either at distribution of the food or getting in a commando where you have a little bit easier. I mean, like I got-- I didn't fight on it. I  was assigned to it, maybe because I looked like maybe 14 years old-- maybe less. And for everything you had to fight-- for your life, for a place to lie down in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got later, you know, the blankets, everybody stole it. You know how everybody pushed when you have 10-- 1 blanket. The food, the work, the extreme weather, if-- I tell you something. Then later, the selection. You know what it meant coming from work, staying in line, and selection when-- not only on the ramps when they came, but new arrivals came. They needed room. People, you know, who didn't look so good, who already failed on their health, they couldn't stand straight. Or you had marks, you know, bruises around your-- everything. You had to undress. And then, the SS from the rows-- people-- I mean, girls-- who were in camp already a year, six months-- worn out. So they just came out [NON-ENGLISH] from-- from-- from the rows, and separate, and to-- and they were gone. It was a special block in Birkenau. It called the block 25. When the selection were-- daily. But they had to have at least 1,000 or more. So it was a [NON-ENGLISH] block, you know, where they concentrated those poor, dying, exhausted, unfortunate in one barrack. And then at night, or even in the day, trucks came and took them straight to the gas chamber. But this was later on, because the first gassing was-- you know when-- I forgot to mention there. I'm so confused and so excited about it. When we-- the first-- end of August, when we came to Birkenau, people who were already sick-- and were sitting there, and couldn't walk the five kilometers-- they were taken by truck. And they told them they were taken by truck, because, you know, they can't walk. Those were the first girls who were gassed-- in August 1942. But in August 1942, I think so-- in Birkenau, the first two crematoriums, one and two, were already finished. By the way, you know, Fred was one of the builders of the crematorium. And so those girls were the first of-- of the gassing, I mean, who already were there six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred. Who is Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred is my husband who was three years also in Auschwitz. But naturally, you know, men and women were not-- in-- in-- in the concentration camps-- separated and in different sections.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you a question at this point. When you had such a will to live, not knowing what is happening from day to day, how did you have that will to live with that uncertainty?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had that will to live, because I always felt the world didn't care about us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I couldn't imagine that people coming from all sides of Europe, that they didn't know where we ended up. And I thought-- I was sure. But they even told us, they says, that the only way to freedom for us is through the crematorium-- through the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we knew that survival there is impossible. But I always said, I wait for tomorrow. Why should I go today? Maybe-- maybe something will change. And some by miracle, we will get out of this hell. So I went day by day. And I said, I never will give up. Although, I tell you, I was so sick. I had malaria. I had dysentery. But in Auschwitz, to survive, you have also to have close friends who take care of each other. You know, they schlepped me to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But luckily, my case, I went to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- to the working place where, you know, sorting the clothing. And they-- dug a-- a hole in, you know, the clothing-- where-- the barracks were full of clothing up to the ceiling. So they dug a hole and were hiding me there. And they worked for me, for which I had to make-- let's say it was a norm-- 20 packages an hour or so. So they worked for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the time was-- at the soup time, they didn't count us. When the time was to go back-- and I slept there. Even I had fever. And they brought me to drink something always. You know how? Because the clothing, when we unpacked, they were thrown in the barracks. It was not sorted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we had to pull-- let's see. I was doing only underwear. So I have to find my underwear in the pile of clothing. So you had to go in on those pile and pull only my part of thing. So that's the reason they could hide girls there. Because each had to go up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely none-- absolutely none-- no medical attention-- nothing, nothing. It was a [NON-ENGLISH]. It means a field hospital. Who went there never came back. But it was-- and they didn't have any medication. They didn't have nothing. You know, the-- the-- the doctors who-- who-- who were there, were doctors who were brought later elderly women-- elderly. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They were already the first year doctors. But for someone who is 18 and someone who is 25 or 30, it means old. You can't be called this time. So you know, they were there helpless. They couldn't help them. You know what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They could-- but one thing-- they didn't have to go to appell-- to that say appell, I meant, to the roll call. And they were laying there on those, you know, bunks, and had a cot-- I mean, a blanket. And hidden there maybe they got some tea. That was the whole thing.
